finding: When features covary perfectly during learning, response requires both features; when covariance is disrupted, response improves for individually presented features. Feature binding operates as a function of feature correlation, not dimension diagnosticity.

Directed forgetting magnitude increases for strong items only when strength is established through context-multiplying operations (spacing); item-strength increases (depth, time) alone produce equivalent forgetting across strength levels. Forgetting depends on context binding, not item accessibility.

Numerical information in different notations is automatically translated to common magnitude representation, evidenced by congruency effects in physical (non-numerical) comparison tasks and interference during physical matching judgments.

Serial position curves for intrusion probes parallel those for positive probes rather than show opposite curvature, supporting dual-process theory with bidirectional recollection routes. Intrusion errors reflect strength-dependent recollection, not familiarity-based false acceptance.

Stereotype-consistent errors are accompanied by illusory remember responses even when dont-know option is available and participants are warned against guessing. False memories for source arise from constructive recollection, not from biased guessing.

Recall decreases as function of task-switch count; concurrent memory load does not modulate switch cost magnitude. Switch-induced attention occupation impairs maintenance via time-based resource sharing, with effect proportional to switching duration regardless of parallel maintenance demands.
